response:
Call update_projects with next_review_date set to the target date.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-abc", "next_review_date": "2026-04-15"}])

This directly sets when the project next appears for review, independent of last_reviewed or the review interval.

Call update_projects with completed_by_children=True.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-xyz", "completed_by_children": true}])

This enables automatic project completion when all child tasks are completed.

No tool call needed — the data is already available from get_tasks. The nextDueDate field on recurring tasks shows the next occurrence's due date without completing the current one.

Call: get_tasks(query="Weekly team standup", recurring_only=True)

Look at the nextDueDate field in the returned task data.

Use the stalled_only filter on get_projects.

Call: get_projects(stalled_only=True)

This returns active projects with no available next actions — exactly what you need to review for stuck or empty projects.

No tool call needed for the explanation — this depends on the catchUpAutomatically field. First, check the task:

Call: get_tasks(query="Take vitamins", recurring_only=True)

Look at the catchUpAutomatically field:
- If true: completing it spawns only one catch-up occurrence (not 5 separate overdue tasks)
- If false: each missed interval spawns its own occurrence, so you may see multiple

The repetitionMethod also matters — "fixed" keeps the original schedule, while "due_after_completion" shifts from today.

No tool call needed — this is a knowledge/explanation question.

If the 'Priority' parent tag has childrenAreMutuallyExclusive set to true, then assigning 'Low' via add_tags will silently remove 'High'. You won't get both — OmniFocus automatically removes the sibling.

To check, call: get_tags() and look at the Priority tag's childrenAreMutuallyExclusive field.

If it's true, update_tasks([{"id": "<task-id>", "add_tags": ["Low"]}]) will replace 'High' with 'Low' automatically. If it's false, both tags will coexist.

Call create_projects with due_date and defer_date.

create_projects([{"name": "Q3 Report", "folder_path": "Work", "due_date": "2026-07-15", "defer_date": "2026-06-01"}])

Call update_projects with due_date set to empty string to clear it.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-abc", "due_date": ""}])

Call get_projects with the project ID.

get_projects(project_id="proj-xyz")

The returned data includes dueDate, deferDate, and plannedDate fields.

Use update_tasks to move the task by setting parent_task_id.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-101", "parent_task_id": "task-200"}])

This makes task-101 a subtask of task-200, moving it out of the inbox.

Use get_tasks with a query search.

get_tasks(query="mortgage")

This searches task names and notes across all projects.

Call get_tags to retrieve all tags with their hierarchy.

get_tags()

Look for the tag named 'High' in the results. The parentTagId field will show if it's nested under another tag. Cross-reference that parentTagId with the 'Energy' tag's id to confirm.

Use update_tasks with status='dropped' to abandon the task without deleting it.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-500", "status": "dropped"}])

Use update_projects with status='dropped' for all three.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-A", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-B", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-C", "status": "dropped"}])

No tool call needed — this is expected behavior explained in the server instructions.

Tasks in a completed project still show completed=false because the completed field reflects the task's own state, not its container's. However, those tasks will have available=false because they inherit unavailability from the completed project.

To avoid seeing them, use get_tasks(available_only=True), which filters to only actionable tasks and excludes tasks in completed projects.

Use reorder_project to move proj-C before proj-A.

reorder_project(project_id="proj-C", before_project_id="proj-A")

Two steps: create the parent tag with mutual exclusivity, then create child tags.

1. create_tags([{"name": "Priority", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}])
2. create_tags([{"name": "High", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Medium", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Low", "parent_tag": "Priority"}])

The parent must exist before creating children that reference it by name.

Use update_tags to enable mutual exclusivity on the existing parent tag.

update_tags([{"id": "tag-energy-001", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}])

This makes it so assigning one child energy tag automatically removes any sibling energy tags from the task.

Use update_projects to flag and tag the project in one call.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-website-001", "flagged": true, "add_tags": ["High Priority"]}])

Use reorder_task to move QA testing before Frontend build.

reorder_task(task_id="task-qa-001", before_task_id="task-fe-001")

Use delete_tags to permanently remove the tag. Tasks lose the tag association but are not deleted.

delete_tags(tag_ids="tag-errands-001")

Call get_perspectives to list all perspectives.

get_perspectives()

The results include a type field — filter for type='custom' to see only custom perspectives.

Use create_folders with parent_path.

create_folders([{"name": "Q2", "parent_path": "Work"}])

Use switch_perspective.

switch_perspective(perspective_name="Weekly Review")

Two-step process:

1. First, get all overdue tasks:
   get_tasks(overdue=True)

2. After reviewing the results and identifying the 3 most urgent task IDs, flag them:
   update_tasks([{"id": "<id1>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id2>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id3>", "flagged": true}])

First, search for the project to confirm it exists:

get_projects(query="Quantum Computing Research")

If found, use the returned project_id to get its tasks:
get_tasks(project_id="<returned_project_id>")

If no project is found, inform the user that no matching project exists.

Use create_tasks with tags as a list.

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Buy birthday gift", "tags": ["Computer", "Errands"]}])

No project_id or parent_task_id means it goes to the inbox.

Use create_tasks with all 5 tasks in one batch call (no project_id = inbox).

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Buy groceries"}, {"task_name": "Do laundry"}, {"task_name": "Wash dishes"}, {"task_name": "Vacuum living room"}, {"task_name": "Prep meals for the week"}])

Use create_projects with both projects in one batch call.

create_projects([{"name": "Kitchen Renovation", "project_type": "sequential", "folder_path": "Home"}, {"name": "Garden Planning", "project_type": "parallel", "folder_path": "Home"}])

Two steps: first create the parent task as a sequential action group, then add subtasks.

1. create_tasks([{"task_name": "Event Planning", "project_id": "proj-100", "sequential": true}])

2. Using the returned parent task ID:
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Research venues", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-id>"}, {"task_name": "Book venue", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-id>"}, {"task_name": "Send invitations", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-id>"}])

The sequential flag on the parent ensures tasks must be done in order.

This requires a parallel project with two sequential action groups to model the dependency chains.

1. Create the parallel project:
   create_projects([{"name": "Home Renovation", "project_type": "parallel"}])

2. Create two sequential action group parent tasks in the project:
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Contractor Track", "project_id": "<project-id>", "sequential": true}, {"task_name": "Paint Track", "project_id": "<project-id>", "sequential": true}])

3. Add subtasks to each action group:
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Get contractor quotes", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-track-id>"}, {"task_name": "Schedule contractor", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-track-id>"}, {"task_name": "Choose paint colors", "parent_task_id": "<paint-track-id>"}, {"task_name": "Buy paint", "parent_task_id": "<paint-track-id>"}])

Since the project is parallel, both tracks run concurrently. Within each sequential action group, the second task is blocked until the first is complete.

Three steps to build the nested structure:

1. Create the top-level sequential action group:
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "project_id": "proj-200", "sequential": true}])

2. Create the sequential subtasks, with 'Create slides' set as a parallel action group:
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Research topic", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Write outline", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Create slides", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>", "sequential": false}])

3. Add parallel subtasks under 'Create slides':
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Design template", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add content", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add animations", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}])

Note: 'Create slides' has sequential=false so its subtasks are parallel, while the parent 'Prepare presentation' is sequential so Research → Outline → Create slides happen in order.

OmniFocus does not support true diamond dependencies (multiple predecessors converging). The closest approximation uses a sequential action group.

Approach: Create a sequential action group where the two independent tasks are in a parallel sub-group, followed by the dependent task.

1. Create a sequential parent action group in the project:
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Report Process", "project_id": "proj-300", "sequential": true}])

2. Create a parallel sub-group for the independent tasks:
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Independent Work", "parent_task_id": "<report-process-id>", "sequential": false}])

3. Add the independent tasks as parallel subtasks, then the dependent task after the sub-group:
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Draft report", "parent_task_id": "<independent-work-id>"}, {"task_name": "Collect data", "parent_task_id": "<independent-work-id>"}])
   create_tasks([{"task_name": "Review findings", "parent_task_id": "<report-process-id>"}])

In the sequential parent, 'Independent Work' (with its parallel subtasks) must complete before 'Review findings' becomes available. Set completed_by_children=true on 'Independent Work' so it auto-completes when both subtasks finish.

Per the server instructions: dropping a recurring task without clearing recurrence first spawns the next occurrence. You must clear recurrence first, then drop.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-weekly-123", "recurrence": "", "status": "dropped"}])

This clears the recurrence rule and drops the task in a single call, preventing any new occurrence from spawning.

Use update_tags to move the tag under a new parent by name.

update_tags([{"id": "tag-aimee-001", "parent_tag": "People"}])

This moves the tag under the 'People' parent. All existing task associations are preserved — only the tag's position in the hierarchy changes.
